ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general canned fruit class (FOODON_03301710); no cherry-specific canned-in-syrup term exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers canned cherries; syrup-packed variant is the closest defensible match within the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for canned cherry in syrup as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of canned cherries in syrup is approximately 80 g (drained weight), equivalent to roughly 6–8 cherries, as commonly consumed as a dessert topping or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Canned Cherry, in Syrup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F04030012 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this item and is set to 0.0 as confirmed trace/negligible per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09067 (Cherries, sour, red, canned, heavy syrup pack, solids and liquids), which reports 0.02 g saturated fat per 100 g — rounded to 0.0 for this processed product.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for canned cherries in heavy syrup in the University of Sydney GI Database. Fresh cherries have a published GI of approximately 22 (low). The addition of heavy sugar syrup raises the effective glycaemic response. Canned fruit in heavy syrup is commonly estimated at GI 55 in clinical nutrition literature (analogous to canned peaches in heavy syrup GI ~58, University of Sydney GI Database). A conservative estimate of GI 55 is applied. Per the University of Sydney GI classification system, GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'. Glycaemic load per 80 g serving = (55 × 17.0 × 0.80) / 100 = 7.5 g. This estimation is documented per Project Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar (15.8 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (0.9 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention despite very low sodium content (4 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is negligible at 4 mg/100 g, the high added sugar from heavy syrup (15.8 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and endothelial dysfunction per AHA 2018 guidelines; patients with hypertension should limit portions to 50 g drained or choose cherries canned in water or natural juice.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 15.8 g/100 g with dietary fibre only 0.9 g/100 g; estimated GI 55 (low boundary) but glycaemic load is clinically significant at serving sizes above 50 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canned cherries in heavy syrup are not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to high added sugar (15.8 g/100 g) and low fibre (0.9 g/100 g); if consumed, limit to 50 g drained portions and prefer fresh or frozen cherries (GI ~22) as a lower-glycaemic alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar content (15.8 g/100 g) from heavy syrup contributes to elevated triglycerides and adverse lipid profiles with regular consumption; fat and saturated fat are negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The negligible fat (0.0 g/100 g) and very low sodium (4 mg/100 g) are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high added sugar (15.8 g/100 g) from heavy syrup may raise triglycerides with regular consumption; individuals with CVD should limit intake and choose water-packed or fresh cherries instead.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (15.8 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators, overriding the natural anthocyanin anti-inflammatory benefit of cherries
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While cherries naturally contain anthocyanins with anti-inflammatory properties, the high added sugar in syrup-packed canned cherries (15.8 g/100 g) promotes inflammation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; choose fresh, frozen, or unsweetened tart cherry juice to access anti-inflammatory benefits without the sugar burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned cherry in syrup is a commercially processed fruit product consisting of whole or halved cherries preserved in heavy sugar syrup, typically containing 15–17% added sugar. The cherries are bright red, soft in texture, and sweet with a mild tartness characteristic of the fruit. The heavy syrup significantly elevates the sugar content compared to fresh cherries (which contain approximately 12–13 g sugar per 100 g naturally), making this product nutritionally distinct from its fresh counterpart. Per 100 g, the product provides 71 kcal, 0.7 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 17.0 g carbohydrate, 15.8 g sugar, and only 0.9 g dietary fibre. Sodium is very low at 4 mg/100 g. Iron content is notable at 1.42 mg/100 g. The product is plant-based, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, the high added sugar and low fibre make this product unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension when consumed in standard portions. The estimated glycaemic index is 55 (low by University of Sydney classification, GI ≤55), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 7.5 per 80 g serving. Fresh or frozen cherries are consistently recommended as lower-glycaemic alternatives. Canned cherries in syrup are commonly used as dessert toppings, in baking, or as a snack, and are widely available in supermarkets globally.
common_user_queries: Are canned cherries in syrup healthy?
How much sugar is in canned cherries?
Is canned cherry good for hypertension?
Difference between fresh and canned cherries nutrition
Can I eat canned cherries if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F04030012, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Source match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate documented per policy, which reduces GI confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong nutrient provenance.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source for this processed canned fruit product; cherries in syrup contain no added fat and the natural fat content of cherries is below detection threshold (confirmed zero). Cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based fruit product with no animal-derived ingredients. Saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 as confirmed trace per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09067 (Cherries, sour, red, canned, heavy syrup pack) which reports 0.02 g/100 g — rounded to 0.0. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions not reported by HPB for this item and not available in the primary source record. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data beyond those reported by HPB (potassium, calcium, iron) are not available in the HPB source record for this specific item; USDA SR Legacy entry 09067 provides partial micronutrient data but was not used to overwrite HPB primary values to preserve source integrity. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on canned peaches in heavy syrup (GI ~58, University of Sydney GI Database) and clinical nutrition literature; no direct GI measurement for canned cherries in heavy syrup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Per the University of Sydney GI classification, GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'; gi_category corrected to 'low' in iteration 3 to align with the GI value of 55. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 is an approximate match (general canned fruit class); FoodEx2 A00LC is an approximate match for canned cherries. No SNOMED CT code assigned — ontology honesty preserved.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' per G5 hard gate requirements.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to align with glycemic_index value of 55. Per University of Sydney GI classification system, GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'. Updated gi_source notes and llm_training description to reflect this correction. Updated type2_diabetes key_factor to reference 'low boundary' GI. Updated notes field to document the correction.
